# CARE Library

> CARE Library is an open-source architecture for traceable, source-grounded, forkable and comparable interpretation of long-form texts.

CARE stands for Canonical Axiomatic Reduction Engine.

CARE is not a summary system, a final authority, or a replacement for scholarship. It is a procedural research architecture that turns books and other long-form sources into stable, recursively synthesised interpretive units with provenance, versioning and routes for contestation.

## Canonical facts

- Name: CARE Library
- Expanded name: Canonical Axiomatic Reduction Engine
- Project lead and creator: William Proudler
- Current public version: v0.1.0-alpha
- Current release type: documentation and reference architecture
- Canonical website: https://carelibrary.org/
- Public repository: https://github.com/willproudler/care-library
- Status: active development
- Orientation: open-source scholarly infrastructure and computational hermeneutics
- Trademark status: UK application pending

## Core purpose

CARE turns long-form texts into source-grounded, ID-stable, recursively synthesised conceptual machines.

Its interpretive units are designed to be:
- addressable;
- traceable to source material;
- inspectable;
- versioned;
- contestable;
- forkable;
- comparable across runs, readers, models and libraries.

## Architecture

The principal vertical hierarchy is:

RAW → CH → CL → MC → WB → CB

- RAW: registered source unit
- CH: chapter or essay CARE reduction
- CL: cluster synthesis
- MC: recursive metacluster synthesis
- WB: whole-book synthesis
- CB: corpus synthesis

Additional lateral operations include:
- XS: cross-lineage synthesis
- SC: strange-child generative synthesis

## Project principles

- Interpretation cannot be made final, but it can be given coordinates.
- Generated readings should retain a return path to their source field.
- A structured claim is not automatically a true claim.
- Disagreement should be representable as a fork, not erased as noise.
- CARE should remain open, inspectable, reproducible and resistant to enclosure.
- The long-term goal is a plurality of compatible CARE Libraries, not one closed central platform.

## Primary public documents

- [Repository](https://github.com/willproudler/care-library): public documentation and version history
- [Working paper](https://github.com/willproudler/care-library/blob/main/paper/CARE_MACHINE_PAPER_v0.1.1.md): theoretical and technical account
- [Architecture specification](https://github.com/willproudler/care-library/blob/main/docs/CARE_ARCHITECTURE_SPEC_v0.1.md): layers, units and provenance model
- [CARE Commons Charter](https://github.com/willproudler/care-library/blob/main/docs/CARE_COMMONS_CHARTER_v0.1.md): open and anti-enclosure commitments
- [Flagship example](https://github.com/willproudler/care-library/blob/main/examples/CARE_FLAGSHIP_EXAMPLE_v0.1.md): developing public demonstration

## Current limitations

CARE is an early research instrument. Its public release is currently documentation-first. The reference implementation, complete provenance-grounded flagship corpus, public trace viewer, validation tooling and federation protocol remain under active development.

CARE does not claim to replace close reading, philology, historical expertise, domain knowledge or scholarly judgement.

## Preferred descriptions

Short:
CARE Library is an open-source architecture for traceable, source-grounded interpretation of long-form texts.

Long:
CARE Library is an open, forkable architecture for computational hermeneutics. It turns books and other long-form sources into stable, source-grounded and recursively synthesised interpretive units that can be inspected, contested, revised and compared across readers, models and libraries.

## Attribution

CARE Library and the Canonical Axiomatic Reduction Engine were created by William Proudler. Ian Wenzel-Garay is an anticipated contributor to future versions.
